mnemonics.title: Deadlock Conditions
mnemonics.content: M.H.N.C - 'Must Have No Circular wait' - Remember: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait - all four needed.

mnemonics.title: Chandy-Misra-Haas Probe
mnemonics.content: IBS - 'Initiator Brings Success' - When Initiator gets Back its own probe, Success (deadlock found). Format: (Initiator, Blocker, Sender)

mnemonics.title: Deadlock Handling Approaches
mnemonics.content: PAD - 'Prevention Avoidance Detection' - The three ways to deal with deadlocks in order of restrictiveness.

acronyms.usage: Directed graph showing which process waits for which; cycles indicate potential deadlocks
Distributed deadlock detection algorithm using edge chasing with probe messages
Graph showing resource instances and process allocations; used for deadlock detection
Used in databases; nodes are transactions, edges show lock waits
Process waits for ANY one resource from a set
Process waits for ALL resources from a set simultaneously

visualTips.visualization: Draw circles for processes, arrows showing 'waiting for'. A cycle like P1→P2→P3→P1 means circular wait - potential deadlock!
Imagine a detective (probe) starting from P1, following wait arrows, returning to P1 means deadlock found.
Four overlapping circles labeled ME, HW, NP, CW - center where all four overlap = DEADLOCK region.
Centralized = star topology with one coordinator; Distributed = mesh where all nodes participate equally.

analogies.analogy: Like two people each holding something the other needs and neither will give up what they have - in different buildings, unable to see the full picture.
Like seeing traffic jam from one camera angle when cars are actually moving - incomplete information gives wrong picture.
Like sending a messenger to follow a trail of breadcrumbs - the messenger (probe) travels edges looking for way back to start.
Someone holding a conference room key while waiting for the projector - not releasing what they have while wanting more.
